skill.name: autonomous-agent-patterns
skill.description: Design patterns for autonomous coding agents, including tool APIs, permission systems, browser automation, context handling, checkpoints, and MCP integrations.
skill.summary: Plan and review autonomous coding agent architectures with practical safety patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The scanned skill is documentation-only and contains no standalone executable scripts or install hooks. Most static findings are Markdown code examples, but several examples show risky patterns if copied directly, including shell execution, broad file access, network ingestion, screenshots, and hot-loading LLM-generated MCP code. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe shell execution pattern in documentation
medium_findings.description: The sandboxing example validates only the first command token, then runs the full command with shell=True. A copied implementation could allow shell metacharacter injection even when the base command is allowlisted. This is documentation code, so it is not active by itself.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly passes a string command to subprocess.run with shell=True after shallow validation. Confidence is reduced because the file is instructional Markdown rather than executable package code.

medium_findings.title: Broad filesystem access patterns lack complete boundary checks
medium_findings.description: Several examples read, write, or collect files from caller-controlled paths. Some nearby text discusses workspace validation, but the read, edit, context, checkpoint, and generated server examples do not consistently enforce that boundary.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The affected examples show direct file access from dynamic paths. They are credible risks if copied, but no active malicious file operation exists in the skill itself.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted network and browser content can enter model context
medium_findings.description: The browser and context examples fetch URLs, capture screenshots, and add remote content into model context. Without origin controls, size limits, prompt-injection filtering, and privacy review, copied agents could expose sensitive browsing state or follow hostile web instructions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly ingest remote content and screenshots into agent state. The risk is architectural rather than confirmed exfiltration, so confidence is high but not definitive.

medium_findings.title: LLM-generated MCP server code is written and hot-loaded
medium_findings.description: The MCP pattern generates server code from a user description, writes it to disk, and connects it immediately. A production implementation would need sandboxing, review, signing, dependency controls, and permission checks before loading generated code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sample flow explicitly creates code, writes it, and hot-loads it. The finding is limited to copied implementations because the repository does not execute this code.

low_findings.title: Static external-command detections are mostly Markdown code fences
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick detections correspond to Markdown fence markers and illustrative blocks, not Ruby command execution. They should not be treated as active shell execution in this skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are visible Markdown fences or documentation examples. No Ruby source file or executable backtick expression was found.

low_findings.title: Weak-cryptography detections appear to be false positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography on lines that contain metadata, prose, schema fields, or ordinary words. No evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or password hashing logic was found in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found prose and example schema text, not cryptographic implementation. The only hash reference is a comment about file hashes.

low_findings.title: No prompt injection attempt found in skill text
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find override language, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, or instructions to skip security analysis. The skill content describes agent design patterns rather than attempting to control the evaluator.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Searches for common prompt-injection phrases found no matching malicious instructions. Confidence is high because the scanned package contains one file.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run with shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample sandbox uses subprocess.run with shell=True on a dynamic command string. This is a known command injection pattern if copied without stronger parsing and argument isolation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact subprocess.run call and shell=True argument are present. It is documented sample code, not executed by the skill package.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hot-loading generated tool code
dangerous_patterns.description: The MCP example writes LLM-generated code to a server file and connects it immediately. This pattern can introduce arbitrary code execution in downstream agent implementations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flow from generated code to file write to server connection is explicit. The risk applies to implementers who copy the pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote content inserted into prompts
dangerous_patterns.description: The context manager fetches URL content and formats it directly into model context. Copied agents should treat that content as untrusted and isolate it from system instructions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code fetches remote content and adds it to prompt context. It does not show malicious behavior, but the architectural risk is clear.

content.value_statement: Autonomous coding agents need clear loops, tool boundaries, and user approval paths. This skill provides patterns for agent architecture, tool design, browser automation, context management, and MCP integration.
actual_capabilities: Explains the think, decide, act, and observe loop for coding agents.
Shows tool schema, file editing, and command execution interface patterns.
Outlines permission levels and approval flows for risky agent actions.
Describes browser automation and visual page interaction patterns.
Covers context injection, checkpoints, resume flows, and MCP tool discovery.
limitations: Provides design examples, not a complete production agent framework.
Example code needs security hardening before direct use in production.
Does not provide working tests, packages, or runnable setup scripts.
Model names and APIs may need updates for a specific provider.

use_cases.title: Plan a Coding Agent Architecture
use_cases.description: Use the patterns to define the loop, model roles, tool list, and safety boundaries for a new coding assistant.

use_cases.title: Review Agent Tool Permissions
use_cases.description: Compare planned tools against the approval and sandboxing patterns before enabling file edits or shell commands.

use_cases.title: Design Browser-Based Agent Workflows
use_cases.description: Use browser and visual-agent examples to plan page navigation, screenshots, text extraction, and user-visible actions.

prompt_templates.title: Map My Agent Loop
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the autonomous-agent-patterns skill to outline a simple coding agent loop for my project. Include the agent steps, required tools, and user approval points.

prompt_templates.title: Design Tool Permissions
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the autonomous-agent-patterns skill to classify these tools by permission level: read files, edit files, run tests, install packages, open URLs, and delete files.

prompt_templates.title: Review Browser Automation Risks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the autonomous-agent-patterns skill to review a browser automation agent design. Focus on screenshots, remote content, selectors, user privacy, and prompt injection risks.

prompt_templates.title: Specify MCP Tool Governance
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the autonomous-agent-patterns skill to propose a governance model for dynamic MCP tools. Include generation review, sandboxing, permissions, audit logging, and rollback.

output_examples.input: Design an agent that edits files and runs tests with user approval.
output_examples.output: A structured architecture summary with an agent loop, approved tools, permission levels, sandbox rules, and a human approval workflow.

output_examples.input: Review whether my browser agent should send screenshots to a vision model.
output_examples.output: A risk review covering screenshot sensitivity, user consent, origin controls, redaction, retention limits, and safer alternatives.

output_examples.input: Plan dynamic MCP tool creation for an internal coding assistant.
output_examples.output: A governance checklist for generated tools, including code review, sandbox execution, dependency policy, audit logs, and rollback steps.

best_practices: Treat file writes, shell commands, network access, and generated code as approval-gated actions.
Keep browser content and remote documents clearly separated from trusted system instructions.
Add audit logs, rollback paths, and workspace boundaries before enabling autonomous changes.
anti_patterns: Do not copy shell=True command execution examples into production without strict argument isolation.
Do not let untrusted web pages or files override system instructions or permission rules.
Do not hot-load generated tools without review, sandboxing, and dependency controls.

faq.question: Is this a runnable agent framework?
faq.answer: No. It is a design guide with illustrative examples for building autonomous coding agents.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The concepts apply to Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and similar tool-using coding agents.

faq.question: Does the skill include security controls?
faq.answer: It describes permission, approval, and sandboxing patterns, but implementers must harden the examples.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level medium?
faq.answer: The file is documentation-only, but several examples show risky patterns if copied into production unchanged.

faq.question: What should I review before using the examples?
faq.answer: Review command execution, file path boundaries, network access, screenshot handling, generated code, and prompt-injection defenses.

faq.question: Who is this skill best for?
faq.answer: It is best for engineers, security reviewers, and platform teams designing autonomous coding assistants.
